phrasal_verbs.meaning: suddenly become unconscious; make a place dark by turning off all the lights, blackout (n)
become conscious again after being unconscious; happen again; go to a place where someone is, to visit them; be persuaded to change your opinion or decision
begin to realise or understand something
laugh a lot, or make someone laugh a lot
check the health of someone who has received medical treatment; try to find out more about something follow-up (n)
make someone feel sad or lose hope
start to feel happy or well again after something bad has happened; solve or deal with a problem
try to hit or attack someone suddenly and violently; speak angrily to or against someone
die (used to avoid saying 'die' when you think this might upset someone)
die (used to avoid saying 'die' when you think this might upset someone)
cause difficulties or pain for someone; behave badly
manage to stay alive after you have been very ill or very badly injured; succeed in a very difficult situation, or help someone do this
become smaller and thinner and not look fresh and healthy; become weaker or smaller
manage to produce a quality or a reaction that helps you deal with a difficult situation
do something to prevent someone or something from harming you

idioms.meaning: waste time feeling upset about something bad that has happened and cannot be changed
suddenly become extremely angry without a good reason

idioms.idiom: give sb a taste/dose of their own medicine
idioms.meaning: treat someone in the same bad way they treat other people
decide not to do something that you usually do
accept a difficult situation without complaining or showing how you feel
used for telling someone not to get angry or upset
be very annoyed because you have made a mistake, missed an opportunity, etc
shout or do something that allows you to get rid of anger
leave a person or situation alone if they might cause you trouble

idioms.idiom: never/don't look a gift horse in the mouth
idioms.meaning: if you are given something good, you should not complain about it or try to find things that are wrong with it

B.title: Emotions in the workplace
B.instructions: Each of the words in bold, except one, is in the wrong place. Write the correct words on the lines.

questions.sentence: Some colleagues tend to (1) ...... **neglect** others behind their backs when something goes wrong, which damages team morale.
Whenever her department exceeds its targets, she can't help but (2) ...... **grimace** over the other teams who fell short.
Even experienced professionals will (3) ...... **conduct** when they're handed an unreasonable workload at the last minute.
When deadlines pile up and communication breaks down, even calm employees grow visibly (4) ...... **impatient**.
The (5) ...... **gloat** of presenting to a hostile boardroom is something most new managers vividly recall.

It is easy to (8) ...... **snap** the training needs of quieter team members when louder voices take up all the attention.
Part of a manager's job is to (9) ...... **contentment** conflict calmly and without taking sides.
Employees are expected to (10) ...... **handle** themselves with professionalism at all company events.

D.title: Circle the correct word or phrase
D.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

questions.sentence: If the hiker has been bitten by a venomous spider, she will need the correct **vaccine / antidote** as quickly as possible.
Doctors now recommend a course of **vaccines / antidotes** before travelling to certain tropical regions.
Are all the children in the programme **inoculated / injected** against measles and rubella?
The rash on his arm is causing constant **irritation / annoyance** and he can't resist scratching it.
I've cut my hand on the glass. Could you please pass me **plaster / a plaster**?
Oliver fractured his wrist during training and his arm has been in **plaster / a plaster** for six weeks.
The specialist's **diagnosis / prognosis** was that the patient had developed a rare autoimmune condition.
Now that the surgery has gone smoothly, her **diagnosis / prognosis** for a full recovery within three months is very positive.
**Preventive / Protective** medicine is centred on keeping people healthy in the first place rather than treating illness once it appears.
My grandfather moved into a nursing **house / home** after he was no longer able to manage on his own.

H.title: Phrasal verbs with through
H.instructions: Tick the phrasal verbs in these sentences which contain the idea of completing something successfully.

questions.sentence: The conditions were appalling, but the expedition team muddled through and reached the summit.
She was flicking through some old magazines when she came across an article about her hometown.
We've committed to this plan and I intend to see it through, whatever happens.

Common sense will win through in the end, even if it takes longer than expected.
The new administration is fully committed to carrying through the promised infrastructure reforms.

M.title: Word formation
M.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

questions.sentence: He became a prominent environmental ………………………………… during his final years at university. ACT
Do you know the ………………………………… of the word 'salary'? DERIVE
I understand it is frustrating, but please try not to ………………………………… – the situation is probably not as serious as it seems. ACT
The physiotherapist asked whether I had any ………………………………… in my fingertips after the procedure. SENSE
A crowd gathered in the square, watching the ………………………………… events with a mixture of horror and disbelief. FOLD
In public health, ………………………………… is widely considered far more cost-effective than treating illness after it occurs. PREVENT
Should it be found that any member of staff has acted ………………………………… , a full disciplinary procedure will be initiated. NEGLECT
Searching for the file after the system crash proved entirely ………………………………… – it had been permanently deleted. FRUIT
The passengers stood ………………………………… on the platform, checking their watches every few seconds as the delay stretched on. PATIENT
After years of careful planning and hard work, the project finally came to ………………………………… last spring. FRUIT

N.title: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space
N.instructions: Use the word given in capitals at the end of the line to form a word that fits in the space in the same line.
N.passage: There are many environmental challenges that remain (1) unresolved in the modern world. Perhaps none is more pressing than the global plastic pollution crisis. Despite decades of research, awareness campaigns and policy proposals, current recycling systems are largely (2) ineffective. A sweeping reduction in the production of single-use plastics appears (3) unavoidable if any meaningful progress is to be achieved. Yet many industries simply cannot operate (4) independently of plastic packaging in their current form, making swift change extremely difficult. Environmental campaigners (5) resolutely push for binding legislative action, pointing out that governments have repeatedly failed to deliver on their promises. Most informed observers would (6) acknowledge that the problem is accelerating faster than the solutions being proposed. To remain (7) insensitive to the full scale of this crisis is no longer a defensible position for any policy maker. Genuine progress will require that scientific (8) knowledge advances hand in hand with genuine political commitment. Any new regulatory framework, and its (9) resulting obligations for industry, must be carefully designed to avoid placing an unfair burden on developing economies. Ultimately, it will fall to future generations to (10) handle the full consequences of the choices we make, or fail to make, today.

questions.sentence: challenges that remain (1) ...... in the modern world. RESOLVE
current recycling systems are largely (2) ...... . EFFECT
A sweeping reduction in single-use plastics appears (3) ...... if progress is to be achieved. AVOID
many industries cannot operate (4) ...... of plastic packaging in their current form. DEPEND
Environmental campaigners (5) ...... push for binding legislative action. RESOLVE
Most informed observers would (6) ...... that the problem is accelerating faster than the solutions. KNOWLEDGE
To remain (7) ...... to the full scale of this crisis is no longer defensible. SENSE
progress will require that scientific (8) ...... advances alongside political commitment. KNOW
Any new regulatory framework and its (9) ...... obligations for industry must be carefully designed. RESULT
it will fall to future generations to (10) ...... the full consequences of the choices we make today. HAND
